questions.question: Why is early return in password comparison a security vulnerability?

options.text: Attacker can determine matching prefix length from timing
It makes the code harder to read

questions.explanation: Early return stops comparing as soon as a difference is found. This means comparing 'Xsecret' takes less time than 'secretX'. An attacker can use timing measurements to determine how many characters match, eventually recovering the entire password one character at a time.

questions.question: What is the correct constant-time pattern for selecting between two values?

options.text: if (condition) return a; else return b;

questions.explanation: The mask-based selection 'mask = -condition; return (a & mask) | (b & ~mask)' executes the same bitwise operations regardless of condition. The other options involve conditional branches or array indexing that can leak through timing or cache behavior.

questions.question: Why is table[secret_index] a timing vulnerability?

options.text: Arrays are slow in all languages
CPU cache behavior reveals which index was accessed
Array bounds checking takes variable time

questions.explanation: When accessing table[index], the CPU loads that memory location into cache. An attacker observing cache state (through cache timing attacks) can determine which index was accessed. Constant-time lookup must touch all table entries to hide the actual access pattern.

questions.question: What is the challenge with floating-point operations in FN-DSA?

options.text: Denormalized numbers and different operations have variable timing

questions.explanation: Floating-point operations have timing variations: denormalized numbers are slower, different operations (add vs multiply) take different times, and results can vary by platform. FN-DSA must use constant-time FP modes or integer-only FFT implementations.

questions.question: How should rejection sampling be implemented in constant time?

options.text: Return immediately when a valid sample is found
Always perform maximum iterations, use first valid sample
Use a random number of iterations
Rejection sampling cannot be made constant-time

questions.explanation: Constant-time rejection sampling always performs the maximum number of iterations, but only uses the first valid sample found. This hides how many attempts were needed. Simply returning on the first valid sample leaks information about the distribution of sampled values.

questions.question: Which tool uses statistical timing analysis to detect leakage?
questions.explanation: DUDECT performs statistical timing analysis using the t-test to detect timing differences between input classes. It's a black-box tool that can validate if an implementation leaks timing information. ctgrind is Valgrind-based but tracks data flow rather than statistical timing.

questions.question: The ternary operator (condition ? a : b) is always constant-time because it's a single expression.
questions.explanation: The ternary operator typically compiles to conditional branches (jump instructions), which have different timing based on the condition. Compilers may optimize it differently on different platforms. Only explicit mask-based selection guarantees constant-time execution.
